findings.title: Hardcoded secret key / password detected
findings.description: Hardcoded credentials in source code can be extracted by anyone with access to the repository.
findings.remediation:
Store secrets in environment variables or a secrets manager:
  os.environ.get('DB_PASSWORD')

findings.title: Use of MD5 hashing algorithm
findings.description: MD5 is cryptographically broken. Collisions can be generated in seconds.

findings.title: Possible SQL Injection via string formatting
findings.description: Untrusted input used directly in SQL query construction. An attacker can alter the query logic.
findings.code_snippet: query = f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}"
findings.remediation:
Use parameterised queries / prepared statements:
  cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=%s', (user_id,))

findings.title: Possible OS Command Injection
findings.description: shell=True passes the command through the shell, enabling command injection if user input is included.
findings.remediation:
Use subprocess with a list of arguments instead:
  subprocess.run(['ls', '-la'], shell=False)

findings.title: Possible OS Command Injection
findings.description: shell=True passes the command through the shell, enabling command injection if user input is included.
findings.remediation:
Use subprocess with a list of arguments instead:
  subprocess.run(['ls', '-la'], shell=False)

findings.title: Use of eval() — arbitrary code execution risk
findings.description: eval() executes arbitrary Python code from a string. If user input reaches eval(), an attacker can run any code.
findings.remediation: Avoid eval() entirely. Use ast.literal_eval() for safe parsing of Python literals.

findings.title: Use of pickle.loads() — arbitrary code execution risk
findings.description: Unpickling untrusted data executes arbitrary Python code. This is a critical vulnerability.
findings.remediation: Use JSON, MessagePack, or similar safe serialisation formats. Never unpickle untrusted data.
findings.owasp: A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures

findings.title: Use of yaml.load() without Loader — code execution risk
findings.description: yaml.load() with untrusted data can execute arbitrary Python objects.
findings.owasp: A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures

findings.title: Use of random module for security-sensitive operations
findings.description: The random module is not cryptographically secure and should not be used for tokens, passwords, or session IDs.
findings.remediation:
Use the secrets module for cryptographic randomness:
  secrets.token_hex(32)

findings.title: Debug mode enabled in web framework
findings.description: Debug mode exposes stack traces, environment variables, and an interactive console to users.
findings.remediation:
Disable debug mode in production:
  app.run(debug=False)

findings.title: SSL/TLS certificate verification disabled
findings.description: Disabling certificate verification makes HTTPS connections vulnerable to man-in-the-middle attacks.
findings.remediation:
Always keep verify=True (the default). If using self-signed certs, provide the CA bundle path:
  requests.get(url, verify='/path/to/ca.pem')

findings.title: HTTP request made with user-supplied URL — potential SSRF
findings.description: Making HTTP requests to user-supplied URLs can lead to Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF), allowing internal network access.
findings.remediation: Validate and whitelist URLs before making requests. Block internal/private IP ranges.

findings.title: assert statement used for security check
findings.description: assert statements are removed when Python is run with -O (optimised mode), bypassing the check.

findings.title: Bare except clause — catches all exceptions including SystemExit
findings.description: 'except:' silently swallows all exceptions, hiding errors and making debugging and auditing very difficult.

findings.title: subprocess called with shell=True
findings.description: shell=True passes the command through the shell interpreter, enabling injection if any argument contains user input.
findings.remediation:
Use shell=False (default) and pass arguments as a list:
  subprocess.run(['cmd', arg1, arg2])
